content.assessment: Because this proposal explicitly leaves the selection age and track structure unchanged, it does not alter network geography, capacity allocation, or transport requirements at all. From a network-planning lens it is largely orthogonal: the geographic concentration of selective capacity (F-live-school_network_planning-05), the network's proximity design for ordinary secondary schools (F-live-school_network_planning-06), and feeder-school dilution (F-live-school_network_planning-07) all persist unaltered. The lens's own limitation applies directly here: network feasibility (in this case, network non-disruption) does not establish whether compensatory funding achieves educational quality or equity gains, which is the proposal's actual target and lies outside this lens's competence.
opportunities: Because it is network-neutral, it could be layered onto any of the other proposals without conflicting with their capacity or geographic mechanisms
strengths: Requires no network restructuring, avoiding transition risk, capacity shortfalls, or transport disruption entirely
threats: Absent any network change, urban concentration and feeder dilution dynamics may continue to offset compensatory funding gains through continued outflow of higher-performing students
weaknesses: Does not address geographic concentration of selective capacity (F-live-school_network_planning-05)
Does not relieve feeder-school dilution or staffing complications in upper primary grades (F-live-school_network_planning-07)
Leaves the stagnant capacity ceiling (F-live-school_network_planning-03) and its associated oversubscription pressure fully in place
